Military Mar 25, 2026

Drone enters Latvian airspace from Russia, crashes near Kraslava

Latvia’s Air Force has identified a foreign unmanned aerial vehicle entering the country’s airspace from Russia before crashing… The post Drone enters Latvian airspace from Russia, crashes near Kraslava appeared first on AeroTime.

NATO member Latvia's airspace incursion highlights the evolving cross-border unmanned threats challenging Eastern European air defense systems and border security protocols.

"Inexcusable": Why Delta’s CEO Just Stripped Congress Of Their VIP Perks

Lawmakers will have to queue alongside regular passengers.

Delta's removal of congressional flight privileges signals airline leadership's willingness to challenge political relationships, potentially influencing how carriers manage government relations during regulatory negotiations.

Military Mar 25, 2026

How The Northrop Grumman’s B-2 Spirit Cockpit Layout Prioritizes Long-Duration Mission Endurance

A closer look at the ergonomics of the world's first fifth-generation bomber.

The B-2's cockpit design minimizes pilot fatigue during 44-hour missions through specialized seating, automated systems, and optimized control placement—principles applicable to commercial long-haul operations.

Where Safety Risk Management moves from analysis to action — how organizations develop controls under §5.55(c)–(d).

Effective risk control implementation requires organizations to systematically document why accepted residual risks remain tolerable, shifting SRM from theoretical analysis to practical operational accountability.

Commercial Mar 25, 2026

Couches In Economy: United Airlines Brings Premium To All Cabins With New Long-Haul Perk

UA's new Relax Row is rolling out in the main cabin this year.

United's Relax Row extends premium amenities to economy passengers, potentially reshaping cabin revenue models by monetizing comfort upgrades across all fare classes rather than restricting them to first/business.

General Aviation Mar 25, 2026

Kona Storms Flood Oʻahu

Back-to-back subtropical cyclones in March fueled destructive flash flooding on several of the Hawaiian Islands.

Infrastructure disruptions from severe weather events in Hawaii could impact aircraft operations and supply chains serving island communities dependent on air transport.

Commercial Mar 25, 2026

How Much Does It Cost To Fly A Boeing 747-400 For 1 Hour?

The Boeing 747-400 is both more expensive and less expensive to operate than modern aircraft with fuel spikes adding to the cost range.

Operating costs for the 747-400 fluctuate significantly with fuel prices, making it less economically predictable than newer, more fuel-efficient widebodies for airlines planning routes.

Part 5 compliance requires structured risk assessment methodology beyond traditional audits, demanding systematic documentation of hazard identification and mitigation controls.

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

Digitizing borders could free up space to reimagine #PaxEx

Border control and lost baggage are the two remaining “pain points” when it comes to creating seamless air passenger journeys. But smart technology deployment and closer cooperation between airlines, airports and regulators could help resolve these issues and free up enough space to transform the...

Digitizing border processes could recover valuable terminal space currently consumed by immigration infrastructure, enabling airports to reinvest that real estate into premium passenger amenities and commercial services.

See Runway Girl Network →

General Aviation Mar 24, 2026

How Much Does Pilot Training Cost Before A US Airline First Officer Reaches The Flight Deck?

A closer look at the roadmap to the cockpit.

Aspiring airline pilots face $200,000+ in cumulative training costs before their first commercial flight, creating a significant barrier to workforce entry during a critical pilot shortage.

See Simple Flying →

Military Mar 24, 2026

U.S. Considering Deployment of 82nd Airborne Division to the Middle East

Senior officials are reportedly considering the deployment of the Immediate Response Force, which includes a Battalion Combat Team of the 82nd Airborne Division, according to U.S. media. After increasing rumors regarding a deployment of U.S. Army troops being in progress, new reports now mention ...

Military airlift capacity requirements will surge if the 82nd Airborne deploys, creating significant demand for strategic transport aircraft and potential impact on civilian charter availability.

See The Aviationist →

Safety Mar 25, 2026

Flying in the Unforgiving Wire Environment (The Rotorcraft Collective)

Many pilots mistakenly believe they will have sufficient reaction time if they encounter power lines and other wires. Yet wire strikes have been among the top three causes of fatal helicopter accident

Helicopter pilots consistently underestimate wire strike risk despite it ranking among the deadliest accident causes, indicating a critical gap between perceived and actual hazard awareness.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

Premium For Everyone: United Airlines Invests In 3 Aircraft Types To Transform Flying

Since 2019, the legacy carrier has added nearly 40% more premium seats.

United's multi-aircraft premium expansion strategy signals airlines are prioritizing high-yield capacity over overall fleet growth to maximize revenue per available seat.

See Simple Flying →

Military Mar 24, 2026

How The Fairchild Republic A-10 Warthog’s Titanium Bathtub Protects Pilots From Ground-Level Ballistics

The story behind America's flying tank buster.

The A-10's titanium armor cockpit can sustain direct hits from 23mm rounds, making pilot survivability in low-altitude combat zones significantly higher than conventional fighter jets.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

NASA PC-12 Aircraft Makes Move to Support Flight Research Across Agency

A NASA Pilatus PC-12 aircraft will now be based at NASA’s Armstrong Flight Research Center in Edwards, California, in order to support flight research efforts across the agency. The PC-12 was acquired in 2022 by NASA’s Glenn Research Center in Cleveland for use in advanced technology development....

NASA's relocation of its PC-12 from Glenn to Armstrong enables broader cross-agency flight research access while optimizing research infrastructure deployment across geographically distributed facilities.

See NASA →

The San Francisco Bay area is complicated airspace, and right in the middle of it is San Carlos Airport. San Carlos airport is surrounded by terrain, San Francisco International class bravo, Metro-Oak

San Carlos Airport's noise abatement procedures reflect the operational constraints imposed by proximity to Class B airspace and residential areas, requiring pilots to balance safety with community relations.

Airlines are leveraging operational disruptions as leverage points in political negotiations, signaling willingness to curtail premium services when government functions falter.

Air Transat Scraps New 12-Hour Longest Route Before A Single Flight Takes Off

The leisure operator was due to take off from Toronto to Ghana's capital on June 27. But the route has been pulled before the first departure...

Leisure carriers are reassessing ultra-long routes as fuel costs and crew fatigue regulations squeeze thin-margin international expansion economics.

Business Aviation Mar 24, 2026

How Much Does It Cost To Upgrade To Lie-Flat Business Class On Transatlantic Flights?

Booking business class isn't the only way to access lie-flat transatlantic aviation. Most major transatlantic airlines offer upgrades.

Premium cabin upgrades from economy can cost 30-70% less than direct business-class tickets, making lie-flat access more accessible for price-conscious frequent flyers.

See Simple Flying →

Industry Mar 25, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across integrated airline-airport-airspace systems offers near-term emissions reductions without waiting for next-generation aircraft technology maturation.

See MDPI →

A shifting geopolitical landscape is driving huge business opportunities for satcoms but also bringing new supply chain and regulatory challenges, industry executives said March 24 at the Satellite Conference in Washington, D.C. The post Geopolitical shifts bring opportunities and headaches for s...

Satellite operators must navigate competing regulatory frameworks across fragmented markets while capitalizing on geopolitical demand for independent communications infrastructure.

Reminders of Where We’ve Been, Where We’re Going

Three Moon rocks are on display during a March 24, 2026, event where NASA announced a series of transformative agencywide initiatives designed to achieve the National Space Policy and advance American leadership in space. NASA leadership provided updates on mission priorities, including sending t...

NASA's display of lunar samples underscores the agency's commitment to sustained deep-space exploration capabilities that will drive advancement in aerospace technologies and international competitive positioning.

Safety Mar 24, 2026

No Transponder, No Warning: NTSB Details LaGuardia Alert Failure

NTSB says LaGuardia’s surface warning system was "unable to alert."

LaGuardia's surface warning system lacked integration with non-transponder equipped aircraft detection, exposing a critical gap in ground-based safety infrastructure for unequipped or malfunctioning aircraft.

Regulatory Mar 24, 2026

FAA Expands Swift 100R Engine STC

Amended STC broadens engine coverage for the unleaded avgas.

The expanded STC for Swift 100R enables broader aircraft compatibility with unleaded avgas, accelerating industry transition away from leaded fuel dependence.

3 Ways Students Can Get Involved With Artemis

NASA’s Artemis program will establish a sustainable lunar presence, unlock new scientific discoveries, and develop technologies for spaceflight to Mars and beyond – and students can help shape this new era of space exploration. As America launches this new Golden Age of innovation and exploration...

Students engaging with Artemis gain hands-on experience in next-generation spacecraft systems and lunar operations that will define aerospace careers for the next decade.

NTSB: A multitude of factors likely led to deadly LaGuardia collision

The National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) held a second briefing, on March 24, 2026, that offered further details… The post NTSB: A multitude of factors likely led to deadly LaGuardia collision appeared first on AeroTime.

NTSB's multi-factor analysis suggests systemic vulnerabilities in airport operations rather than single-point failures, requiring comprehensive procedural reviews across similar high-traffic environments.

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

NASA Selects Finalists in Student Aircraft Maintenance Competition

Editor’s note: This advisory was updated on March 24, 2026, to update the finalists list. NASA has selected seven student teams as finalists in the 2026 Gateways to Blue Skies Competition, giving them the resources to help address a critical challenge for U.S. aviation: aircraft maintenance. Chal...

NASA's competition targets the aerospace maintenance workforce shortage by engaging students early, potentially creating a pipeline of skilled technicians for the industry.

Drones Mar 24, 2026

ResilienX Receives FAA Waiver for Remote BVLOS Operations

ResilienX, Inc., a leader in aviation safety and BVLOS drone networks, announced today that it

ResilienX's FAA waiver enables beyond-visual-line-of-sight operations without visual observers, significantly reducing deployment costs and expanding commercial drone application feasibility.

Drones Mar 24, 2026

When Wireless Networks Falter, Drones Can Provide Backup Connectivity, According to Stevens Researchers

Researchers at Stevens Institute of Technology have developed a drone-based system designed to provide temporary

Drone swarms can rapidly deploy as resilient communication infrastructure during network outages, enabling emergency response coordination when terrestrial systems fail.

Drones Mar 24, 2026

America’s new 911 drone is always ready

Drones responding to 911 calls are no longer experimental. Across the US, police departments are already using Drone as First Responder (DFR) programs to get eyes on emergencies faster than patrol units. In many cities, drones arrive in under two minutes, well before officers. But even with that ...

DFR programs are demonstrating measurable operational value by achieving sub-2-minute response times, establishing drones as a viable complement to traditional emergency response infrastructure.

See DroneDJ →

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

NASA X-Ray Mission Gets Fresh Look at 2,000-Year-Old Supernova

NASA’s IXPE (Imaging X-ray Polarimetry Explorer) mission has taken a new observation of a supernova, RCW 86, helping fill in a fuller picture of what other telescopes have observed. When astronomers using NASA’s Chandra X-ray Observatory previously targeted RCW 86, they discovered that a large “c...

IXPE's X-ray polarimetry measurements of ancient supernovae provide aerospace engineers with critical validation data for next-generation space telescope designs and high-energy astrophysics instrumentation.

See NASA →

Regulatory Mar 24, 2026

SparkPNT Enters the Next Chapter with Facet FP

Designed with the belief that users should have total control over the devices they own,

Facet FP's user-control focus addresses growing aviation industry concerns about proprietary device limitations and supply chain vulnerabilities in critical positioning systems.

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

Moog taps Redwire to provide solar arrays for Meteor

WASHINGTON — Redwire announced the first award for its new solar arrays, a $12.8 million contract from Moog. Under the contract announced March 24, Redwire will deliver Extensible Low-Profile Solar Array (ELSA) wings for Moog’s Meteor satellite bus ordered by an undisclosed national security cust...

Redwire's commercial solar array technology is securing defense contracts, signaling growing private sector capability in national security space systems.

Safety Mar 24, 2026

NTSB: LaGuardia Surface Alert System Did Not Trigger

Investigators said the fire truck involved did not have a transponder, and a stepped-on radio transmission remains under review.

Equipment gaps and communication failures during ground operations create blind spots that automated safety systems cannot detect, highlighting vulnerability in airport surface management.
